   128	V = Voltage (Volts, V)
   129	I = Current (Amperes, A)
   130	R = Resistance (Ohms, Ω)
   131	\`\`\`
   132	
   133	**Power Triangle:**
   134	\`\`\`
   135	         P
   136	        / \\
   137	       /   \\
   138	      V --- I
   139	      
   140	  • Cover P → V × I
   141	  • Cover V → P ÷ I
   142	  • Cover I → P ÷ V
   143	\`\`\`
   144	
   145	**Detailed Example 5: Power Calculation**
   146	*A heater draws 8A from a 230V supply. How much power does it consume?*
   147	
   148	\`\`\`
   149	Step 1: Identify known values
   150	Voltage (V) = 230V
   151	Current (I) = 8A
   152	
   153	Step 2: Apply power formula
   154	P = V × I
   155	P = 230 × 8
   156	P = 1,840W
   157	
   158	Answer: 1,840 Watts (1.84 kW)
   159	\`\`\`
   160	
   161	**Detailed Example 6: Power from Resistance**
   162	*A 20Ω resistor has 5A flowing through it. Calculate the power dissipated.*
   163	
   164	\`\`\`
   165	Method 1: P = I² × R = 5² × 20 = 25 × 20 = 500W
   166	Method 2: First find V = I × R = 5 × 20 = 100V, then P = V × I = 100 × 5 = 500W
   167	
   168	Answer: 500 Watts
   169	\`\`\`
   170	
   171	**Detailed Example 7: Energy Consumption**
   172	*A 2kW heater runs for 3 hours. How much energy does it consume?*
   173	
   174	\`\`\`
   175	Step 1: Identify known values
   176	Power (P) = 2kW
   177	Time (t) = 3 hours
   178	
   179	Step 2: Apply energy formula
   180	Energy = Power × Time
   181	Energy = 2kW × 3h
   182	Energy = 6 kWh
   183	
   184	Answer: 6 kilowatt-hours
   185	\`\`\`
   186	
   187	**Cost Calculation:**
   188	\`\`\`
   189	If electricity costs ₦100 per kWh:
   190	Cost = 6 kWh × ₦100 = ₦600
   191	Answer: ₦600
   192	\`\`\`
   193	
   194	**Detailed Example 8: Energy Cost**
   195	*A 100W light bulb is left on for 24 hours. If electricity costs ₦95/kWh, what is the cost?*
   196	
   197	\`\`\`
   198	Energy = 100W × 24h = 2,400 Wh = 2.4 kWh
   199	Cost = 2.4 × 95 = ₦228
   200	Answer: ₦228
   201	\`\`\`
   202	
   203	---
   204	
   205	### Series and Parallel Circuits
   206	
   207	#### Series Circuits
   208	
   209	In a series circuit, components are connected end-to-end in a single path.
   210	
   211	\`\`\`
   212	Characteristics:
   213	- Same current flows through all components
   214	- Total resistance = Sum of individual resistances
   215	  (R_Total = R₁ + R₂ + R₃ + ...)
   216	- Voltage divides across components
   217	- Total voltage = Sum of voltage drops
   218	- If one component fails, the circuit is broken
   219	\`\`\`
   220	
   221	**Shortcut: Voltage Divider Rule**
   222	\`\`\`
   223	Vx = V_Total × (Rx / R_Total)
   224	
   225	Where:
   226	Vx = Voltage across resistor x
   227	Rx = Resistance of resistor x
   228	R_Total = Total resistance
   229	\`\`\`
   230	
   231	**Detailed Example 9: Series Circuit**
   232	*Three resistors: 10Ω, 20Ω, and 30Ω are connected in series to a 60V supply. Find:*
   233	*a) Total resistance*
   234	*b) Circuit current*
   235	*c) Voltage across each resistor using voltage divider rule*
   236	
   237	\`\`\`
   238	Step 1: Total resistance
   239	R_Total = R₁ + R₂ + R₃
   240	R_Total = 10 + 20 + 30 = 60Ω
   241	
   242	Step 2: Circuit current
   243	I = V ÷ R_Total
   244	I = 60 ÷ 60 = 1A
   245	
   246	Step 3: Voltage drops using Voltage Divider Rule
   247	V₁ = V_Total × (R₁/R_Total) = 60 × (10/60) = 10V
   248	V₂ = V_Total × (R₂/R_Total) = 60 × (20/60) = 20V
   249	V₃ = V_Total × (R₃/R_Total) = 60 × (30/60) = 30V
   250	
   251	Check: 10V + 20V + 30V = 60V ✓
   252	
   253	Answer: a) 60Ω, b) 1A, c) 10V, 20V, 30V
   254	\`\`\`
   255	
   256	#### Parallel Circuits
   257	
   258	In a parallel circuit, components are connected across each other, providing multiple paths.
   259	
   260	\`\`\`
   261	Characteristics:
   262	- Same voltage across all components
   263	- Total current = Sum of branch currents
   264	  (I_Total = I₁ + I₂ + I₃ + ...)
   265	- Total resistance formula:
   266	  1/R_Total = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + 1/R₃ + ...
   267	- Total resistance is less than the smallest individual resistance
   268	- If one component fails, others continue working
   269	\`\`\`
   270	
   271	**Shortcut: Two Resistors in Parallel**
   272	\`\`\`
   273	R_Total = (R₁ × R₂) / (R₁ + R₂)
   274	
   275	(Only for TWO resistors in parallel)
   276	\`\`\`
   277	
   278	**Shortcut: Equal Resistors in Parallel**
   279	\`\`\`
   280	If n equal resistors (R) are in parallel:
   281	R_Total = R / n
   282	\`\`\`
   283	
   284	**Shortcut: Current Divider Rule**
   285	\`\`\`
   286	Ix = I_Total × (R_Total / Rx)
   287	
   288	Where:
   289	Ix = Current through branch x
   290	Rx = Resistance of branch x
   291	R_Total = Total resistance
   292	\`\`\`
   293	
   294	**Detailed Example 10: Parallel Circuit**
   295	*Three resistors: 10Ω, 20Ω, and 30Ω are connected in parallel across a 60V supply. Find:*
   296	*a) Total resistance using shortcuts*
   297	*b) Total current*
   298	*c) Current through each resistor*
   299	
   300	\`\`\`
   301	Step 1: Total resistance
   302	1/R_Total = 1/10 + 1/20 + 1/30
   303	1/R_Total = 6/60 + 3/60 + 2/60 = 11/60
   304	R_Total = 60/11 = 5.45Ω
   305	
   306	Step 2: Total current
   307	I_Total = V ÷ R_Total
   308	I_Total = 60 ÷ 5.45 = 11A
   309	
   310	Step 3: Branch currents using Current Divider Rule
   311	I₁ = I_Total × (R_Total/R₁) = 11 × (5.45/10) = 6A
   312	I₂ = I_Total × (R_Total/R₂) = 11 × (5.45/20) = 3A
   313	I₃ = I_Total × (R_Total/R₃) = 11 × (5.45/30) = 2A
   314	
   315	Check: 6A + 3A + 2A = 11A ✓
   316	
   317	Answer: a) 5.45Ω, b) 11A, c) 6A, 3A, 2A
   318	\`\`\`
   319	
   320	**Detailed Example 11: Two Resistors in Parallel**
   321	*Calculate total resistance of 4Ω and 6Ω resistors in parallel.*
   322	
   323	\`\`\`
   324	Using shortcut: R_Total = (R₁ × R₂)/(R₁ + R₂)
   325	R_Total = (4 × 6)/(4 + 6) = 24/10 = 2.4Ω
   326	
   327	Answer: 2.4Ω
   328	\`\`\`
   329	
   330	**Detailed Example 12: Equal Resistors in Parallel**
   331	*Four 10Ω resistors are connected in parallel. What is the total resistance?*
   332	
   333	\`\`\`
   334	Using shortcut: R_Total = R/n = 10/4 = 2.5Ω
   335	
   336	Answer: 2.5Ω
   337	\`\`\`
   338	
   339	---
   340	
   341	### Three-Phase Systems
   342	
   343	Three-phase power is the standard for industrial and commercial electrical systems.
   344	
   345	\`\`\`
   346	In a three-phase system:
   347	- Three alternating currents are generated, 120° apart
   348	- Used for high-power applications
   349	- More efficient than single-phase power
   350	- Constant power delivery (no pulsations)
   351	\`\`\`
   352	
   353	#### Three-Phase Power Formulas
   354	
   355	\`\`\`
   356	Apparent Power (kVA) = √3 × V_Line × I_Line
   357	Active Power (kW) = √3 × V_Line × I_Line × PF
   358	Reactive Power (kVAR) = √3 × V_Line × I_Line × sin(θ)
   359	
   360	Where:
   361	V_Line = Line-to-line voltage
   362	I_Line = Line current
   363	PF = Power Factor
   364	θ = Angle between voltage and current
   365	\`\`\`
   366	
   367	#### Star (Wye) Connection
   368	
   369	\`\`\`
   370	V_Line = √3 × V_Phase
   371	I_Line = I_Phase
   372	
   373	Where:
   374	V_Line = Voltage between two lines
   375	V_Phase = Voltage between a line and neutral
   376	I_Line = Current in a line
   377	I_Phase = Current in a phase
   378	
   379	Power in Star: P = √3 × V_Line × I_Line × PF
   380	\`\`\`
   381	
   382	**Detailed Example 13: Star Connection**
   383	*A 3-phase star-connected system has a phase voltage of 230V. Find the line voltage.*
   384	
   385	\`\`\`
   386	Step 1: Identify known values
   387	V_Phase = 230V
   388	
   389	Step 2: Apply formula
   390	V_Line = √3 × V_Phase
   391	V_Line = 1.732 × 230
   392	V_Line = 398.4V ≈ 400V
   393	
   394	Answer: 400V (approximately)
   395	\`\`\`
   396	
   397	**Detailed Example 14: Star Connection Power**
   398	*A 400V, 3-phase star-connected system supplies a load with line current of 50A and power factor 0.85. Find the active power.*
   399	
   400	\`\`\`
   401	P = √3 × V_Line × I_Line × PF
   402	P = 1.732 × 400 × 50 × 0.85
   403	P = 1.732 × 400 × 42.5
   404	P = 29,444W ≈ 29.4kW
   405	
   406	Answer: 29.4 kW
   407	\`\`\`
   408	
   409	#### Delta Connection
   410	
   411	\`\`\`
   412	V_Line = V_Phase
   413	I_Line = √3 × I_Phase
   414	
   415	Where:
   416	V_Line = Voltage between two lines
   417	V_Phase = Voltage across a phase
   418	I_Line = Current in a line
   419	I_Phase = Current in a phase
   420	
   421	Power in Delta: P = √3 × V_Line × I_Line × PF
   422	\`\`\`
   423	
   424	**Detailed Example 15: Delta Connection**
   425	*A 3-phase delta-connected system has a line voltage of 415V. Find the phase voltage.*
   426	
   427	\`\`\`
   428	Step 1: Identify known values
   429	V_Line = 415V
   430	
   431	Step 2: Apply formula
   432	V_Phase = V_Line
   433	V_Phase = 415V
   434	
   435	Answer: 415V
   436	\`\`\`
   437	
   438	**Detailed Example 16: Delta Current**
   439	*In a delta-connected system, the phase current is 50A. Find the line current.*
   440	
   441	\`\`\`
   442	Step 1: Identify known values
   443	I_Phase = 50A
   444	
   445	Step 2: Apply formula
   446	I_Line = √3 × I_Phase
   447	I_Line = 1.732 × 50
   448	I_Line = 86.6A
   449	
   450	Answer: 86.6A
   451	\`\`\`
   452	
   453	**Detailed Example 17: Star-Delta Comparison**
   454	*A load is connected in star with phase voltage 230V. If reconfigured to delta with same phase voltage, what is the line voltage in each configuration?*
   455	
   456	\`\`\`
   457	Star: V_Line = √3 × 230 = 400V
   458	Delta: V_Line = V_Phase = 230V
   459	
   460	Answer: Star line voltage = 400V, Delta line voltage = 230V
   461	\`\`\`
   462	
   463	---
   464	
   465	### Power Factor
   466	
   467	Power factor is the ratio of true power (real power) to apparent power.
   468	
   469	\`\`\`
   470	PF = True Power / Apparent Power
   471	PF = cos(θ)
   472	
   473	Where:
   474	θ = Phase angle between voltage and current
   475	
   476	Values:
   477	- Resistive load: PF = 1 (Unity)
   478	- Inductive load: PF < 1 (Lagging)
   479	- Capacitive load: PF < 1 (Leading)
   480	
   481	Relationship:
   482	True Power = Apparent Power × PF
   483	Apparent Power = True Power / PF
   484	Reactive Power = Apparent Power × sin(θ)
   485	\`\`\`
   486	
   487	**Detailed Example 18: Power Factor Calculation**
   488	*A motor consumes 40 kW of true power and 50 kVA of apparent power. What is the power factor?*
   489	
   490	\`\`\`
   491	Step 1: Identify known values
   492	True Power = 40 kW
   493	Apparent Power = 50 kVA
   494	
   495	Step 2: Apply formula
   496	PF = True Power / Apparent Power
   497	PF = 40 ÷ 50
   498	PF = 0.8
   499	
   500	Answer: 0.8 (lagging - typical for motors)
   501	\`\`\`
   502	
   503	**Detailed Example 19: Finding Apparent Power**
   504	*A motor uses 30kW true power and has a power factor of 0.75. What is the apparent power?*
   505	
   506	\`\`\`
   507	Apparent Power = True Power / PF
   508	Apparent Power = 30 / 0.75 = 40kVA
   509	
   510	Answer: 40 kVA
   511	\`\`\`
   512	
   513	**Detailed Example 20: Reactive Power Calculation**
   514	*For the motor in Example 19, calculate the reactive power.*
   515	
   516	\`\`\`
   517	Step 1: Find sin(θ) from PF
   518	PF = cos(θ) = 0.75
   519	θ = cos⁻¹(0.75) = 41.41°
   520	sin(θ) = sin(41.41°) = 0.661
   521	
   522	Step 2: Calculate reactive power
   523	Reactive Power = Apparent Power × sin(θ)
   524	Reactive Power = 40 × 0.661 = 26.45 kVAR
   525	
   526	Answer: 26.45 kVAR
   527	\`\`\`
   528	
   529	**Why Power Factor Matters:**
   530	
   531	\`\`\`
   532	- Low PF = Higher current for same power
   533	- Higher current = More losses (I²R losses)
   534	- Utilities may charge penalties for low PF
   535	- Power factor correction improves efficiency
   536	- Capacitors are often used to correct PF (capacitive PF correction)
   537	\`\`\`
   538	
   539	**Detailed Example 21: Power Factor Correction**
   540	*A 100kW load has PF = 0.7. What is the current at 400V? What would it be if PF were corrected to 0.95?*
   541	
   542	\`\`\`
   543	Step 1: Current at PF = 0.7
   544	I = P / (√3 × V × PF)
   545	I = 100,000 / (1.732 × 400 × 0.7)
   546	I = 100,000 / 484.96
   547	I = 206.2A
   548	
   549	Step 2: Current at PF = 0.95
   550	I = 100,000 / (1.732 × 400 × 0.95)
   551	I = 100,000 / 658.16
   552	I = 151.9A
   553	
   554	Savings: 206.2A - 151.9A = 54.3A (26.3% reduction)
   555	
   556	Answer: PF=0.7 gives 206A, PF=0.95 gives 152A
   557	\`\`\`
   558	
   559	---
   560	
   561	### Transformers
   562	
   563	Transformers are devices that transfer electrical energy between circuits using electromagnetic induction.
   564	
   565	\`\`\`
   566	V₁/V₂ = N₁/N₂ = I₂/I₁
   567	
   568	Where:
   569	V₁ = Primary voltage
   570	V₂ = Secondary voltage
   571	N₁ = Primary turns
   572	N₂ = Secondary turns
   573	I₁ = Primary current
   574	I₂ = Secondary current
   575	
   576	Transformer Ratios:
   577	Turns Ratio = N₁/N₂ = V₁/V₂
   578	Current Ratio = I₂/I₁ = N₁/N₂
   579	\`\`\`
   580	
   581	**Detailed Example 22: Step-Down Transformer**
   582	*A transformer has 1,000 primary turns and 100 secondary turns. If the primary voltage is 11,000V, what is the secondary voltage?*
   583	
   584	\`\`\`
   585	Step 1: Identify known values
   586	N₁ = 1,000 turns
   587	N₂ = 100 turns
   588	V₁ = 11,000V
   589	
   590	Step 2: Apply formula
   591	V₁/V₂ = N₁/N₂
   592	11,000/V₂ = 1,000/100
   593	11,000/V₂ = 10
   594	V₂ = 11,000/10
   595	V₂ = 1,100V
   596	
   597	Answer: 1,100V (Step-down transformer)
   598	\`\`\`
   599	
   600	**Detailed Example 23: Step-Up Transformer**
   601	*A transformer has 200 primary turns and 2,000 secondary turns. If the primary voltage is 230V, what is the secondary voltage?*
   602	
   603	\`\`\`
   604	V₂ = V₁ × (N₂/N₁)
   605	V₂ = 230 × (2,000/200)
   606	V₂ = 230 × 10 = 2,300V
   607	
   608	Answer: 2,300V (Step-up transformer)
   609	\`\`\`
   610	
   611	**Detailed Example 24: Transformer Current**
   612	*A 10kVA transformer has a primary voltage of 11,000V and secondary voltage of 230V. Find the primary and secondary currents.*
   613	
   614	\`\`\`
   615	Step 1: Calculate primary current
   616	I₁ = VA ÷ V₁
   617	I₁ = 10,000 ÷ 11,000
   618	I₁ = 0.91A
   619	
   620	Step 2: Calculate secondary current
   621	I₂ = VA ÷ V₂
   622	I₂ = 10,000 ÷ 230
   623	I₂ = 43.48A
   624	
   625	Check: V₁/V₂ = I₂/I₁ = 11,000/230 = 47.83
   626	I₂/I₁ = 43.48/0.91 = 47.78 ✓
   627	
   628	Answer: I₁ = 0.91A, I₂ = 43.48A
   629	\`\`\`
   630	
   631	**Detailed Example 25: Transformer Efficiency**
   632	*A transformer has input power of 500W and output power of 475W. Calculate the efficiency.*
   633	
   634	\`\`\`
   635	Efficiency = (Output/Input) × 100%
   636	Efficiency = (475/500) × 100% = 95%
   637	
   638	Answer: 95%
   639	\`\`\`
   640	
   641	**Transformer Losses:**
   642	\`\`\`
   643	1. Core Losses (Iron Losses):
   644	   - Hysteresis loss
   645	   - Eddy current loss
   646	   - Constant regardless of load
   647	
   648	2. Copper Losses:
   649	   - I²R losses in windings
   650	   - Vary with load (proportional to I²)
   651	
   652	3. Total Losses = Core Losses + Copper Losses
   653	\`\`\`
   654	
   655	---
   656	
   657	### Motors
   658	
   659	Electric motors convert electrical energy into mechanical energy.
   660	
   661	#### Synchronous Speed Formula
   662	
   663	\`\`\`
   664	Ns = (120 × f) ÷ P
   665	
   666	Where:
   667	Ns = Synchronous speed (RPM)
   668	f = Frequency (Hz)
   669	P = Number of poles
   670	\`\`\`
   671	
   672	**Detailed Example 26: Synchronous Speed**
   673	*Calculate the synchronous speed of a 4-pole motor operating at 50Hz.*
   674	
   675	\`\`\`
   676	Step 1: Identify known values
   677	f = 50 Hz
   678	P = 4 poles
   679	
   680	Step 2: Apply formula
   681	Ns = (120 × 50) ÷ 4
   682	Ns = 6,000 ÷ 4
   683	Ns = 1,500 RPM
   684	
   685	Answer: 1,500 RPM
   686	\`\`\`
   687	
   688	**Detailed Example 27: Different Poles**
   689	*A motor operates at 50Hz and has a synchronous speed of 1,000 RPM. How many poles does it have?*
   690	
   691	\`\`\`
   692	P = (120 × f) / Ns
   693	P = (120 × 50) / 1,000
   694	P = 6,000 / 1,000 = 6 poles
   695	
   696	Answer: 6 poles
   697	\`\`\`
   698	
   699	**Detailed Example 28: Different Frequency**
   700	*A 6-pole motor is connected to a 60Hz supply. What is its synchronous speed?*
   701	
   702	\`\`\`
   703	Step 1: Identify known values
   704	f = 60 Hz
   705	P = 6 poles
   706	
   707	Step 2: Apply formula
   708	Ns = (120 × 60) ÷ 6
   709	Ns = 7,200 ÷ 6
   710	Ns = 1,200 RPM
   711	
   712	Answer: 1,200 RPM
   713	\`\`\`
   714	
   715	**Detailed Example 29: Slip Calculation**
   716	*A 4-pole, 50Hz induction motor has a full-load speed of 1,440 RPM. Calculate the slip.*
   717	
   718	\`\`\`
   719	Step 1: Calculate synchronous speed
   720	Ns = (120 × 50) / 4 = 1,500 RPM
   721	
   722	Step 2: Calculate slip
   723	Slip = (Ns - N) / Ns × 100%
   724	Slip = (1,500 - 1,440) / 1,500 × 100%
   725	Slip = 60 / 1,500 × 100% = 4%
   726	
   727	Answer: 4% slip
   728	\`\`\`
   729	
   730	**Motor Types:**
   731	
   732	\`\`\`
   733	1. DC Motors:
   734	   - Series, Shunt, Compound wound
   735	   - Speed control by varying voltage/field
   736	   - High starting torque (series)
   737	   - Constant speed (shunt)
   738	
   739	2. AC Motors:
   740	   - Induction (Squirrel cage, Wound rotor)
   741	   - Synchronous motors
   742	   - Most common in industry
   743	   - Squirrel cage: Simple, robust, low cost
   744	   - Wound rotor: High starting torque, speed control
   745	
   746	3. Special Motors:
   747	   - Servo motors (position control)
   748	   - Stepper motors (precise rotation)
   749	   - BLDC motors (high efficiency)
   750	   - Linear motors (straight line motion)
   751	\`\`\`
   752	
   753	---
   754	
   755	### Protection Systems
   756	
   757	#### Circuit Breakers
   758	\`\`\`
   759	Purpose: Protect circuits from:
   760	- Overcurrent
   761	- Short circuits
   762	- Overloads
   763	
   764	Types:
   765	- MCB (Miniature Circuit Breaker): Up to 100A
   766	- MCCB (Molded Case Circuit Breaker): 100-2,500A
   767	- ACB (Air Circuit Breaker): Up to 6,300A
   768	- VCB (Vacuum Circuit Breaker): Up to 36kV
   769	- SF6 Circuit Breaker: High voltage
   770	
   771	Characteristics:
   772	- Tripping curves:
   773	  Type B: 3-5× In (lighting, domestic)
   774	  Type C: 5-10× In (motors, transformers)
   775	  Type D: 10-20× In (heavy industrial)
   776	\`\`\`
   777	
   778	**Detailed Example 30: Breaker Selection**
   779	*A motor draws 50A running current and has a starting current of 300A. Which type of MCB should be used?*
   780	
   781	\`\`\`
   782	Type D (10-20×): 50 × 10 = 500A (can handle 300A starting)
   783	Type C (5-10×): 50 × 5 = 250A (would trip on startup)
   784	
   785	Answer: Type D MCB (10-20× In)
   786	\`\`\`
   787	
   788	#### Fuses
   789	\`\`\`
   790	- One-time protection device
   791	- Melts when current exceeds rating
   792	- Cheaper than breakers
   793	- Must be replaced after operation
   794	- Types:
   795	  - HRC (High Rupturing Capacity) fuse
   796	  - Cartridge fuse
   797	  - Rewirable fuse
   798	\`\`\`
   799	
   800	#### Earthing/Grounding
   801	\`\`\`
   802	Types:
   803	- Equipment Earthing: Protect equipment
   804	- System Earthing: Protect personnel
   805	- Lightning Protection: Dissipate lightning strikes
   806	
   807	Components:
   808	- Earth electrode
   809	- Earth conductor
   810	- Earthing resistance (should be < 1Ω)
   811	
   812	Standards:
   813	- IEC 60364
   814	- BS 7671
   815	- IEEE Standard 80
   816	\`\`\`
   817	
   818	**Detailed Example 31: Earthing Resistance**
   819	*What is the typical acceptable value of earth resistance?*
   820	
   821	\`\`\`
   822	Answer: Less than 1Ω for ideal earthing, up to 5Ω for acceptable earthing
   823	\`\`\`
   824	
   825	#### Surge Protection
   826	\`\`\`
   827	Purpose: Protect against voltage spikes
   828	Sources:
   829	- Lightning strikes
   830	- Switching operations
   831	- Electrostatic discharge
   832	
   833	Types:
   834	- MOV (Metal Oxide Varistor)
   835	- TVS (Transient Voltage Suppressor)
   836	- Gas discharge tubes
   837	- Surge arrestors
   838	
   839	Protection Levels:
   840	- Type 1: Direct lightning strikes
   841	- Type 2: Induced surges
   842	- Type 3: Equipment protection
   843	\`\`\`
   844	
   845	---
   846	
   847	### Electrical Safety
   848	
   849	**Before Working on Electrical Equipment:**
   850	
   851	\`\`\`
   852	1. Isolate the equipment
   853	2. Lock out/tag out (LOTO)
   854	3. Test for voltage
   855	4. Earth/Ground the circuit
   856	5. Wear appropriate PPE
   857	6. Obtain necessary permits
   858	7. Ensure safe work area
   859	\`\`\`
   860	
   861	**Basic Electrical Safety Rules:**
   862	
   863	\`\`\`
   864	- Never work on live equipment
   865	- Always assume circuits are live
   866	- Use insulated tools
   867	- Wear appropriate PPE (gloves, goggles)
   868	- Follow LOTO procedures
   869	- Maintain safe distances
   870	- Use the correct rating of fuses/breakers
   871	- Keep fire extinguishers nearby
   872	- Know emergency procedures
   873	\`\`\`
   874	
   875	**Electrical Shock Response:**
   876	
   877	\`\`\`
   878	First step:
   879	1. Turn off the power source
   880	2. If unable, use non-conductive material to separate victim
   881	3. Call for emergency help
   882	4. Perform CPR if trained and needed
   883	
   884	DO NOT touch the person with bare hands if still in contact!
   885	\`\`\`
   886	
   887	**First Aid for Electrical Burns:**
   888	\`\`\`
   889	1. Stop the burning process
   890	2. Remove burning or restrictive clothing (if not stuck)
   891	3. Cover the burn with clean dressing
   892	4. Do not apply creams or ointments
   893	5. Get medical help immediately
   894	\`\`\`
   895	
   896	---
   897	
   898	### Practice Questions with Detailed Solutions
   899	
   900	**Question 1: Ohm's Law**
   901	*A 12V circuit draws 3A. What is the resistance?*
   902	
   903	\`\`\`
   904	R = V/I = 12/3 = 4Ω
   905	Answer: 4Ω
   906	\`\`\`
   907	
   908	**Question 2: Synchronous Speed**
   909	*Calculate the synchronous speed of a 6-pole motor at 50Hz.*
   910	
   911	\`\`\`
   912	Ns = 120 × f / P = 120 × 50 / 6 = 1,000 RPM
   913	Answer: 1,000 RPM
   914	\`\`\`
   915	
   916	**Question 3: Circuit Breaker Purpose**
   917	*What is the primary purpose of a circuit breaker?*
   918	
   919	\`\`\`
   920	Answer: To protect electrical circuits from overcurrent and short circuits
   921	\`\`\`
   922	
   923	**Question 4: Power Calculation**
   924	*A 230V single-phase motor draws 10A. What is the power?*
   925	
   926	\`\`\`
   927	P = V × I = 230 × 10 = 2,300W = 2.3kW
   928	Answer: 2.3 kW
   929	\`\`\`
   930	
   931	**Question 5: Transformer**
   932	*A transformer has a turns ratio of 10:1 (primary:secondary). If primary voltage is 33kV, what is secondary voltage?*
   933	
   934	\`\`\`
   935	V₂ = V₁ × N₂/N₁ = 33,000 × 1/10 = 3,300V = 3.3kV
   936	Answer: 3.3 kV
   937	\`\`\`
   938	
   939	**Question 6: Energy Consumption**
   940	*A 2.5kW heater runs for 8 hours. Calculate energy consumption and cost at ₦150/kWh.*
   941	
   942	\`\`\`
   943	Energy = 2.5 × 8 = 20 kWh
   944	Cost = 20 × 150 = ₦3,000
   945	Answer: 20 kWh, ₦3,000
   946	\`\`\`
   947	
   948	**Question 7: Parallel Resistance**
   949	*Calculate total resistance of 4Ω and 6Ω resistors in parallel.*
   950	
   951	\`\`\`
   952	Using shortcut: Rt = (4 × 6)/(4 + 6) = 24/10 = 2.4Ω
   953	Answer: 2.4Ω
   954	\`\`\`
   955	
   956	**Question 8: Series Circuit**
   957	*Three resistors 4Ω, 6Ω, and 10Ω are in series. Total resistance?*
   958	
   959	\`\`\`
   960	Rt = 4 + 6 + 10 = 20Ω
   961	Answer: 20Ω
   962	\`\`\`
   963	
   964	**Question 9: Power Factor**
   965	*A motor uses 30kW true power and has a power factor of 0.75. What is apparent power?*
   966	
   967	\`\`\`
   968	Apparent Power = True Power / PF
   969	Apparent Power = 30 / 0.75 = 40kVA
   970	Answer: 40 kVA
   971	\`\`\`
   972	
   973	**Question 10: Star Connection**
   974	*In a star-connected system, line voltage is 415V. What is phase voltage?*
   975	
   976	\`\`\`
   977	V_Phase = V_Line / √3
   978	V_Phase = 415 / 1.732 = 240V
   979	Answer: 240V
   980	\`\`\`
   981	
   982	**Question 11: Slip Calculation**
   983	*A 6-pole, 50Hz motor runs at 960 RPM. Calculate the slip.*
   984	
   985	\`\`\`
   986	Ns = (120 × 50)/6 = 1,000 RPM
   987	Slip = (1,000 - 960)/1,000 × 100 = 4%
   988	Answer: 4%
   989	\`\`\`
   990	
   991	**Question 12: Transformer Current**
   992	*A 5kVA transformer has 230V primary. What is the primary current?*
   993	
   994	\`\`\`
   995	I₁ = 5,000/230 = 21.74A
   996	Answer: 21.74A
   997	\`\`\`
   998	
   999	**Question 13: Motor Poles**
  1000	*A 50Hz motor has synchronous speed of 1,500 RPM. How many poles?*
  1001	
  1002	\`\`\`
  1003	P = (120 × 50)/1,500 = 6,000/1,500 = 4 poles
  1004	Answer: 4 poles
  1005	\`\`\`
  1006	
  1007	**Question 14: Energy Cost**
  1008	*A 1.5kW air conditioner runs for 5 hours. Cost at ₦120/kWh?*
  1009	
  1010	\`\`\`
  1011	Energy = 1.5 × 5 = 7.5 kWh
  1012	Cost = 7.5 × 120 = ₦900
  1013	Answer: ₦900
  1014	\`\`\`
  1015	
  1016	**Question 15: Delta Connection**
  1017	*In a delta system, phase current is 20A. What is line current?*
  1018	
  1019	\`\`\`
  1020	I_Line = √3 × 20 = 34.64A
  1021	Answer: 34.64A
  1022	\`\`\`
  1023	
  1024	---
  1025	
  1026	### Quick Reference: Electrical Formulas
  1027	
  1028	| Quantity | Formula | Units |
  1029	|----------|---------|-------|
  1030	| Voltage | V = I × R | Volts (V) |
  1031	| Current | I = V / R | Amperes (A) |
  1032	| Resistance | R = V / I | Ohms (Ω) |
  1033	| Power (DC) | P = V × I | Watts (W) |
  1034	| Power (AC) | P = V × I × PF | Watts (W) |
  1035	| Energy | E = P × t | Watt-hours (Wh) |
  1036	| Synchronous Speed | Ns = 120f/P | RPM |
  1037	| Transformer | V₁/V₂ = N₁/N₂ | - |
  1038	| Star Connection | V_Line = √3 × V_Phase | Volts |
  1039	| Delta Connection | I_Line = √3 × I_Phase | Amperes |
  1040	| Apparent Power | S = √3 × V_Line × I_Line | VA |
  1041	| Active Power | P = √3 × V_Line × I_Line × PF | W |
  1042	| Reactive Power | Q = √3 × V_Line × I_Line × sin(θ) | VAR |
  1043	
  1044	---
  1045	
  1046	### Common Electrical Engineering Mistakes
  1047	
  1048	 **Wrong**: Using peak voltage in power calculations
  1049	 **Correct**: Use RMS voltage for AC power calculations
  1050	
  1051	 **Wrong**: Forgetting √3 in three-phase power calculations
  1052	 **Correct**: P = √3 × V_Line × I_Line × PF
  1053	
  1054	 **Wrong**: Ignoring power factor in AC circuits
  1055	 **Correct**: P = V × I × PF (not just V × I)
  1056	
  1057	 **Wrong**: Thinking higher voltage is always more dangerous
  1058	 **Correct**: Current kills, not voltage. Both can be dangerous.
  1059	
  1060	 **Wrong**: Using same formulas for AC and DC circuits
  1061	 **Correct**: AC circuits require RMS values and power factor
  1062	
  1063	 **Wrong**: Confusing star and delta connections
  1064	 **Correct**: Star: V_Line = √3 × V_Phase; Delta: I_Line = √3 × I_Phase
  1065	
  1066	 **Wrong**: Forgetting transformer losses
  1067	 **Correct**: Always consider core losses and copper losses
  1068	`
  1069	    },
  1070	    {
  1071	      id: 'general-engineering',
  1072	      title: 'General Engineering',
  1073	      content: `
  1074	### Introduction to General Engineering
  1075	
  1076	General engineering covers the fundamental principles that apply across all engineering disciplines. This includes mechanics, thermodynamics, fluid dynamics, and material science.
  1077	
  1078	---
  1079	
  1080	### Fluid Mechanics
  1081	
  1082	Fluid mechanics is the study of fluids (liquids and gases) at rest and in motion.
  1083	
  1084	#### Hydrostatic Pressure
  1085	
  1086	Pressure at a point in a fluid depends on the depth and density.
  1087	
  1088	\`\`\`
  1089	P = ρ × g × h
  1090	
  1091	Where:
  1092	P = Pressure (Pascal, Pa)
  1093	ρ = Density of fluid (kg/m³)
  1094	g = Gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s²)
  1095	h = Depth (meters)
  1096	
  1097	Alternative form:
  1098	P = γ × h
  1099	Where γ = Specific weight (N/m³)
  1100	
  1101	Pascal's Principle:
  1102	- Pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted equally in all directions
  1103	- P = F/A (Pressure = Force / Area)
  1104	\`\`\`
  1105	
  1106	**Detailed Example 1: Hydrostatic Pressure**
  1107	*Calculate the pressure at the bottom of a tank filled with water to a depth of 10 meters. (Density of water = 1,000 kg/m³)*
  1108	
  1109	\`\`\`
  1110	Step 1: Identify known values
  1111	ρ = 1,000 kg/m³
  1112	g = 9.81 m/s²
  1113	h = 10 m
  1114	
  1115	Step 2: Apply formula
  1116	P = ρ × g × h
  1117	P = 1,000 × 9.81 × 10
  1118	P = 98,100 Pa = 98.1 kPa
  1119	
  1120	Answer: 98.1 kPa (or 0.981 bar)
  1121	\`\`\`
  1122	
  1123	**Detailed Example 2: Pressure in Different Fluids**
  1124	*A tank contains oil with density 900 kg/m³ to a depth of 5 meters. Calculate the pressure at the bottom.*
  1125	
  1126	\`\`\`
  1127	P = 900 × 9.81 × 5 = 44,145 Pa = 44.1 kPa
  1128	Answer: 44.1 kPa
  1129	\`\`\`
  1130	
  1131	**Detailed Example 3: Force from Pressure**
  1132	*What is the force on the bottom of the tank in Example 1 if the tank has an area of 2 m²?*
  1133	
  1134	\`\`\`
  1135	Force = Pressure × Area
  1136	F = 98,100 Pa × 2 m² = 196,200 N = 196.2 kN
  1137	
  1138	Answer: 196.2 kN
  1139	\`\`\`
  1140	
  1141	#### Pressure Units and Conversions
  1142	
  1143	\`\`\`
  1144	Common units:
  1145	1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 14.7 psi = 1.013 bar
  1146	1 bar = 100 kPa = 14.5 psi
  1147	1 psi = 6.895 kPa = 0.0689 bar
  1148	
  1149	Conversions:
  1150	kPa → bar: Divide by 100
  1151	bar → kPa: Multiply by 100
  1152	psi → kPa: Multiply by 6.895
  1153	kPa → psi: Divide by 6.895
  1154	\`\`\`
  1155	
  1156	**Detailed Example 4: Pressure Conversion**
  1157	*Convert 2.5 bar to kPa and to psi.*
  1158	
  1159	\`\`\`
  1160	Step 1: Convert to kPa
  1161	2.5 bar × 100 = 250 kPa
  1162	
  1163	Step 2: Convert to psi
  1164	250 kPa ÷ 6.895 = 36.26 psi
  1165	
  1166	Answer: 250 kPa, 36.26 psi
  1167	\`\`\`
  1168	
  1169	**Detailed Example 5: Conversion Practice**
  1170	*Convert 150 psi to kPa and bar.*
  1171	
  1172	\`\`\`
  1173	kPa = 150 × 6.895 = 1,034.25 kPa
  1174	bar = 1,034.25 ÷ 100 = 10.34 bar
  1175	
  1176	Answer: 1,034 kPa, 10.34 bar
  1177	\`\`\`
  1178	
  1179	#### Fluid Properties
  1180	
  1181	**Viscosity:**
  1182	\`\`\`
  1183	- Resistance to flow
  1184	- High viscosity = Thick fluid (e.g., honey, molasses)
  1185	- Low viscosity = Thin fluid (e.g., water, air)
  1186	- Dynamic viscosity (μ) = Units: Pa·s or N·s/m²
  1187	- Kinematic viscosity (ν) = μ/ρ (Units: m²/s)
  1188	
  1189	Typical Values:
  1190	- Water (20°C): μ = 0.001 Pa·s, ν = 1.0 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s
  1191	- Air (20°C): μ = 1.8 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s
  1192	- Oil (SAE 30): μ ≈ 0.2-0.3 Pa·s
  1193	\`\`\`
  1194	
  1195	**Density:**
  1196	\`\`\`
  1197	- Mass per unit volume
  1198	- Water = 1,000 kg/m³
  1199	- Oil ≈ 800-900 kg/m³
  1200	- Air ≈ 1.225 kg/m³ at sea level
  1201	- Mercury = 13,600 kg/m³
  1202	\`\`\`
  1203	
  1204	**Specific Gravity:**
  1205	\`\`\`
  1206	SG = ρ_fluid / ρ_water
  1207	
  1208	Water: SG = 1.0
  1209	Oil: SG ≈ 0.8-0.9
  1210	Mercury: SG = 13.6
  1211	\`\`\`
  1212	
  1213	**Detailed Example 6: Specific Gravity**
  1214	*What is the specific gravity of a fluid with density 800 kg/m³?*
  1215	
  1216	\`\`\`
  1217	SG = 800 ÷ 1,000 = 0.8
  1218	Answer: 0.8
  1219	\`\`\`
  1220	
  1221	**Detailed Example 7: Density from SG**
  1222	*A fluid has specific gravity 1.25. What is its density?*
  1223	
  1224	\`\`\`
  1225	ρ = SG × ρ_water = 1.25 × 1,000 = 1,250 kg/m³
  1226	Answer: 1,250 kg/m³
  1227	\`\`\`
  1228	
  1229	---
  1230	
  1231	### Pumps
  1232	
  1233	Pumps are devices that move fluids by mechanical action.
  1234	
  1235	#### Types of Pumps
  1236	
  1237	**Positive Displacement Pumps:**
  1238	\`\`\`
  1239	- Move a fixed volume per cycle
  1240	- Handle viscous fluids well
  1241	- Can produce high pressures
  1242	- Constant flow at different pressures
  1243	- Examples: Gear pump, piston pump, diaphragm pump
  1244	- Efficiency: 85-95%
  1245	
  1246	Best for:
  1247	- High viscosity fluids
  1248	- High pressure applications
  1249	- Precise flow control
  1250	- Metering applications
  1251	- Shear-sensitive fluids (use diaphragm pump)
  1252	\`\`\`
  1253	
  1254	**Centrifugal Pumps:**
  1255	\`\`\`
  1256	- Use rotating impeller to add energy
  1257	- Best for low pressure, high flow
  1258	- Not suitable for viscous fluids
  1259	- Flow decreases as pressure increases
  1260	- Examples: End suction, multistage, submersible
  1261	- Efficiency: 60-85%
  1262	
  1263	Best for:
  1264	- Low viscosity fluids
  1265	- High flow rates
  1266	- Clean fluids
  1267	- Low pressure applications
  1268	- Large volume transfer
  1269	\`\`\`
  1270	
  1271	**Pump Selection Criteria:**
  1272	\`\`\`
  1273	1. Fluid properties (viscosity, corrosiveness, temperature)
  1274	2. Required flow rate (Q)
  1275	3. Required discharge pressure (H)
  1276	4. Suction conditions (NPSH required vs available)
  1277	5. Operating temperature
  1278	6. Efficiency requirements
  1279	7. Cost considerations
  1280	8. Maintenance requirements
  1281	\`\`\`
  1282	
  1283	**Pump Performance Curve:**
  1284	\`\`\`
  1285	Key parameters:
  1286	- Flow rate (Q) in m³/h or gpm
  1287	- Head (H) in meters or feet
  1288	- Efficiency (η) in percentage
  1289	- Power (P) in kW or hp
  1290	
  1291	Relationship:
  1292	Power = (Q × H × ρ × g) / (η × 1000)  [kW]
  1293	Power (hp) = (Q × H) / (3,960 × η)  [US units]
  1294	
  1295	Where:
  1296	Q = Flow rate (m³/s)
  1297	H = Head (m)
  1298	ρ = Density (kg/m³)
  1299	g = 9.81 m/s²
  1300	η = Efficiency (decimal)
  1301	\`\`\`
  1302	
  1303	**Detailed Example 8: Pump Power Calculation**
  1304	*A pump delivers 0.1 m³/s of water with a head of 50m. The pump efficiency is 80%. Calculate the power required.*
  1305	
  1306	\`\`\`
  1307	P = (Q × H × ρ × g) / (η × 1000)
  1308	P = (0.1 × 50 × 1,000 × 9.81) / (0.8 × 1000)
  1309	P = (49,050) / (800)
  1310	P = 61.3 kW
  1311	
  1312	Answer: 61.3 kW
  1313	\`\`\`
  1314	
  1315	**Detailed Example 9: Pump Power in HP**
  1316	*Convert the answer from Example 8 to horsepower.*
  1317	
  1318	\`\`\`
  1319	1 hp = 0.746 kW
  1320	P = 61.3 / 0.746 = 82.2 hp
  1321	
  1322	Answer: 82.2 hp
  1323	\`\`\`
  1324	
  1325	---
  1326	
  1327	### Compressors
  1328	
  1329	Compressors increase the pressure of gases.
  1330	
  1331	#### Types of Compressors
  1332	
  1333	**Positive Displacement Compressors:**
  1334	\`\`\`
  1335	- Reciprocating: Piston type
  1336	- Rotary: Screw, vane, lobe
  1337	- Handle high pressure ratios (up to 10:1 per stage)
  1338	- Suitable for small to medium flows
  1339	- Efficiency: 70-85%
  1340	
  1341	Advantages:
  1342	- High pressure capability
  1343	- Suitable for various gases
  1344	- Good efficiency at high pressures
  1345	\`\`\`
  1346	
  1347	**Dynamic Compressors:**
  1348	\`\`\`
  1349	- Centrifugal: Radial flow
  1350	- Axial: Axial flow
  1351	- High flow rates (10,000+ m³/h)
  1352	- Lower pressure ratios per stage (1.5:1 to 2:1)
  1353	- Efficiency: 80-90%
  1354	
  1355	Advantages:
  1356	- High flow rates
  1357	- Smooth operation
  1358	- Low maintenance
  1359	- Can handle large volumes
  1360	\`\`\`
  1361	
  1362	**Compressor Components:**
  1363	\`\`\`
  1364	Intercooler: Cools air between stages (reduces work by ~15%)
  1365	Aftercooler: Cools air after compression (removes moisture)
  1366	Separator: Removes oil and water
  1367	Dryer: Removes moisture (prevents corrosion in pipes)
  1368	
  1369	Purpose of Intercooler:
  1370	- Reduces temperature between stages
  1371	- Decreases compression work
  1372	- Prevents overheating
  1373	- Increases efficiency
  1374	\`\`\`
  1375	
  1376	**Detailed Example 10: Compressor Pressure Ratio**
  1377	*A compressor takes in air at 101 kPa and discharges at 1,010 kPa. What is the pressure ratio?*
  1378	
  1379	\`\`\`
  1380	Pressure Ratio = Discharge Pressure / Suction Pressure
  1381	Pressure Ratio = 1,010 / 101 = 10
  1382	
  1383	Answer: 10:1
  1384	\`\`\`
  1385	
  1386	**Detailed Example 11: Multi-Stage Compression**
  1387	*If the pressure ratio of 10:1 is achieved with two stages, what is the pressure ratio per stage?*
  1388	
  1389	\`\`\`
  1390	Per Stage Ratio = √10 = 3.16:1
  1391	Answer: 3.16:1 per stage
  1392	\`\`\`
  1393	
  1394	---
  1395	
  1396	### Heat Transfer
  1397	
  1398	Heat transfer is the movement of thermal energy from one object or area to another.
  1399	
  1400	#### Three Modes of Heat Transfer
  1401	
  1402	**1. Conduction:**
  1403	\`\`\`
  1404	- Through solid materials
  1405	- Direct contact transfer
  1406	- Fourier's Law: Q = -kA(dT/dx)
  1407	- Thermal conductivity (k) varies by material
  1408	
  1409	Good conductors: Metals (copper: 401 W/m·K, aluminum: 237 W/m·K)
  1410	Poor conductors (insulators): Air (0.026 W/m·K), wood (0.12 W/m·K), fiberglass (0.04 W/m·K)
  1411	
  1412	Examples:
  1413	- Heat through a metal bar
  1414	- Heat through a wall
  1415	- Cooking pan handle getting hot
  1416	- Heat loss through pipes
  1417	\`\`\`
  1418	
  1419	**Detailed Example 12: Conduction Heat Transfer**
  1420	*A copper rod (k=401 W/m·K) has cross-sectional area 0.01m² and length 1m. If temperature difference is 50°C, what is heat transfer?*
  1421	
  1422	\`\`\`
  1423	Q = -kA(ΔT/L) = -401 × 0.01 × (50/1)
  1424	Q = -200.5 W (negative indicates heat flow)
  1425	
  1426	Answer: 200.5 W (magnitude)
  1427	\`\`\`
  1428	
  1429	**2. Convection:**
  1430	\`\`\`
  1431	- Through fluids (liquids or gases)
  1432	- Movement of fluid carries heat
  1433	- Newton's Law: Q = hA(T_surface - T_fluid)
  1434	
  1435	Types:
  1436	- Natural Convection: Due to density differences (no external force)
  1437	- Forced Convection: Assisted by pumps or fans
  1438	
  1439	Examples:
  1440	- Boiling water
  1441	- Air conditioner cooling a room
  1442	- Radiator heating a room
  1443	- Cooling of electronic components
  1444	\`\`\`
  1445	
  1446	**3. Radiation:**
  1447	\`\`\`
  1448	- Through electromagnetic waves
  1449	- No medium required
  1450	- Can travel through vacuum
  1451	- Stefan-Boltzmann Law: Q = εσAT⁴
  1452	- Emissivity (ε) varies by surface
  1453	
  1454	Examples:
  1455	- Sun warming the earth
  1456	- Heat from a fire
  1457	- Infrared heaters
  1458	- Thermal imaging cameras
  1459	
  1460	Radiation properties:
  1461	- Black body: ε = 1 (perfect absorber/emitter)
  1462	- White/reflective surfaces: ε ≈ 0.1-0.3
  1463	- Dark/rough surfaces: ε ≈ 0.8-0.95
  1464	\`\`\`
  1465	
  1466	**Detailed Example 13: Heat Transfer Identification**
  1467	*A hot cup of coffee cools down. What modes of heat transfer are involved?*
  1468	
  1469	\`\`\`
  1470	1. Conduction: Heat through the cup to your hand
  1471	2. Convection: Heat from the surface to surrounding air
  1472	3. Radiation: Heat radiated from the surface
  1473	
  1474	Answer: All three modes
  1475	\`\`\`
  1476	
  1477	---
  1478	
  1479	### Mechanical Systems
  1480	
  1481	#### Efficiency
  1482	
  1483	Efficiency measures how well a system converts input energy to useful output.
  1484	
  1485	\`\`\`
  1486	Efficiency = (Output Power / Input Power) × 100%
  1487	
  1488	Losses = Input - Output
  1489	Losses include: Friction, heat, sound, vibration, wear
  1490	
  1491	Typical Efficiencies:
  1492	- Electrical motor: 85-96%
  1493	- Internal combustion engine: 25-40%
  1494	- Transformer: 95-98%
  1495	- Pump: 60-85%
  1496	- Compressor: 70-85%
  1497	- LED light: 30-50% (much better than incandescent at 5-10%)
  1498	\`\`\`
  1499	
  1500	**Detailed Example 14: Efficiency Calculation**
  1501	*A machine has an input power of 5 kW and output power of 4 kW. What is the efficiency?*
  1502	
  1503	\`\`\`
  1504	Efficiency = (4 kW / 5 kW) × 100%
  1505	Efficiency = 0.8 × 100 = 80%
  1506	
  1507	Answer: 80%
  1508	\`\`\`
  1509	
  1510	**Detailed Example 15: Finding Input from Efficiency**
  1511	*A motor is 85% efficient and produces 3.4 kW of mechanical power. What is the input power?*
  1512	
  1513	\`\`\`
  1514	Input = Output / Efficiency
  1515	Input = 3.4 kW / 0.85
  1516	Input = 4 kW
  1517	
  1518	Answer: 4 kW
  1519	\`\`\`
  1520	
  1521	**Detailed Example 16: Finding Losses**
  1522	*If the motor in Example 15 has input power 4kW and output 3.4kW, what are the losses?*
  1523	
  1524	\`\`\`
  1525	Losses = Input - Output
  1526	Losses = 4 - 3.4 = 0.6 kW = 600 W
  1527	
  1528	Answer: 600 W losses
  1529	\`\`\`
  1530	
  1531	#### Mechanical Advantage
  1532	
  1533	\`\`\`
  1534	Mechanical Advantage = Output Force / Input Force
  1535	
  1536	For pulleys:
  1537	MA = Number of rope segments supporting the load
  1538	
  1539	For levers:
  1540	MA = Distance from effort to fulcrum / Distance from load to fulcrum
  1541	
  1542	For gears:
  1543	MA = Number of teeth on driven gear / Number of teeth on driving gear
  1544	
  1545	For inclined plane:
  1546	MA = Length of incline / Height of incline
  1547	
  1548	Ideal Mechanical Advantage (IMA) = Perfect/no friction
  1549	Actual Mechanical Advantage (AMA) = Includes friction
  1550	Efficiency = AMA / IMA
  1551	\`\`\`
  1552	
  1553	**Detailed Example 17: Mechanical Advantage - Lever**
  1554	*A lever has the effort arm 2 meters from the fulcrum and the load arm 0.5 meters from the fulcrum. What is the mechanical advantage?*
  1555	
  1556	\`\`\`
  1557	MA = 2 / 0.5 = 4
  1558	
  1559	This means you can lift 4 times the force you apply.
  1560	If you apply 100N, you can lift 400N.
  1561	Answer: 4
  1562	\`\`\`
  1563	
  1564	**Detailed Example 18: Mechanical Advantage - Pulley**
  1565	*A block and tackle system has 4 rope segments supporting the load. What is the mechanical advantage?*
  1566	
  1567	\`\`\`
  1568	MA = 4
  1569	Answer: 4
  1570	\`\`\`
  1571	
  1572	**Detailed Example 19: Mechanical Advantage - Gears**
  1573	*A gear system has 40 teeth on the driving gear and 120 teeth on the driven gear. What is the mechanical advantage?*
  1574	
  1575	\`\`\`
  1576	MA = 120/40 = 3
  1577	Answer: 3
  1578	\`\`\`
  1579	
  1580	---
  1581	
  1582	### Materials Engineering
  1583	
  1584	#### Properties of Materials
  1585	
  1586	**Mechanical Properties:**
  1587	\`\`\`
  1588	- Strength: Ability to withstand load without failure
  1589	  - Yield Strength: Where permanent deformation begins
  1590	  - Ultimate Tensile Strength: Maximum stress before failure
  1591	- Hardness: Resistance to indentation (scratching, cutting)
  1592	- Ductility: Ability to deform without fracture (wire drawing)
  1593	- Brittleness: Tendency to fracture without deformation
  1594	- Toughness: Ability to absorb energy without fracturing
  1595	- Fatigue: Resistance to repeated loading cycles
  1596	- Creep: Time-dependent deformation under constant stress
  1597	\`\`\`
  1598	
  1599	**Detailed Example 20: Material Property Identification**
  1600	*What property would you consider if selecting a material for a spring?*
  1601	
  1602	\`\`\`
  1603	Answer: Fatigue resistance (for repeated loading) and yield strength (to prevent permanent deformation)
  1604	\`\`\`
  1605	
  1606	**Material Classifications:**
  1607	\`\`\`
  1608	1. Metals:
  1609	   - Ferrous: Steel (strong, magnetic, prone to rust)
  1610	   - Cast iron (hard, brittle, good compression)
  1611	   - Non-ferrous: Aluminum (light, corrosion resistant)
  1612	   - Copper (excellent conductor), Brass (decorative), Bronze (bearings)
  1613	   - Properties: Strong, conductive, ductile
  1614	
  1615	2. Polymers:
  1616	   - Plastics: Thermoplastics (melt on heating), Thermosets (permanent setting)
  1617	   - Rubbers: Elastomers (flexible, high elasticity)
  1618	   - Properties: Lightweight, insulating, flexible
  1619	
  1620	3. Ceramics:
  1621	   - Glass, bricks, tiles, porcelain
  1622	   - Properties: Hard, brittle, heat resistant, chemical resistant
  1623	
  1624	4. Composites:
  1625	   - Carbon fiber (high strength-to-weight ratio)
  1626	   - Fiberglass (good strength, corrosion resistant)
  1627	   - Reinforced plastics (various properties)
  1628	   - Properties: High strength-to-weight ratio, customized properties
  1629	\`\`\`
  1630	
  1631	**Detailed Example 21: Material Selection**
  1632	*What material would you select for a car body panel?*
  1633	
  1634	\`\`\`
  1635	Considerations:
  1636	- Light weight: Aluminum or composite
  1637	- Strength: Steel or carbon fiber
  1638	- Cost: Steel (cheapest), Aluminum (moderate), Carbon fiber (expensive)
  1639	- Formability: Steel (good), Aluminum (good), Carbon fiber (limited)
  1640	
  1641	Common choice: Steel for economy cars, Aluminum for luxury/performance, Carbon fiber for racing
  1642	
  1643	Answer: Depends on requirements - steel (cost), aluminum (weight), or carbon fiber (performance)
  1644	\`\`\`
  1645	
  1646	**Corrosion:**
  1647	\`\`\`
  1648	- Deterioration of metals due to chemical reactions
  1649	- Most common: Rust (iron oxide formation)
  1650	- Types: Galvanic, crevice, pitting, uniform, stress corrosion
  1651	
  1652	Prevention Methods:
  1653	- Coating: Paint, plating, galvanizing
  1654	- Material selection: Stainless steel, corrosion-resistant alloys
  1655	- Cathodic protection: Sacrificial anodes, impressed current
  1656	- Inhibitors: Chemicals that slow corrosion
  1657	- Design: Avoid crevices, provide drainage
  1658	\`\`\`
  1659	
  1660	**Detailed Example 22: Corrosion Prevention**
  1661	*Name two methods to prevent corrosion in pipelines.*
  1662	
  1663	\`\`\`
  1664	1. Coating: Paint or epoxy coating
  1665	2. Cathodic protection: Sacrificial anodes or impressed current
  1666	
  1667	Answer: Any two from the list above
  1668	\`\`\`
  1669	
  1670	---
  1671	
  1672	### Thermodynamics
  1673	
  1674	#### Laws of Thermodynamics
  1675	
  1676	**Zeroth Law:**
  1677	\`\`\`
  1678	If two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, they are in thermal equilibrium with each other.
  1679	(Basis for temperature measurement)
  1680	\`\`\`
  1681	
  1682	**First Law: Conservation of Energy**
  1683	\`\`\`
  1684	Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted from one form to another.
  1685	ΔU = Q - W
  1686	
  1687	Where:
  1688	ΔU = Change in internal energy (J)
  1689	Q = Heat added to system (J)
  1690	W = Work done by system (J)
  1691	
  1692	Sign Convention:
  1693	- Q positive = Heat added to system
  1694	- W positive = Work done by system
  1695	\`\`\`
  1696	
  1697	**Second Law: Entropy**
  1698	\`\`\`
  1699	- Heat cannot spontaneously flow from cold to hot
  1700	- Entropy of an isolated system always increases
  1701	- No heat engine can be 100% efficient
  1702	- Carnot efficiency: η = 1 - T_cold/T_hot
  1703	
  1704	Carnot Efficiency Example:
  1705	- Hot reservoir: 400°C (673K)
  1706	- Cold reservoir: 25°C (298K)
  1707	- η_max = 1 - 298/673 = 1 - 0.443 = 0.557 = 55.7%
  1708	- No real engine can exceed this efficiency
  1709	\`\`\`
  1710	
  1711	**Third Law: Absolute Zero**
  1712	\`\`\`
  1713	As temperature approaches absolute zero (0K or -273.15°C), entropy approaches a minimum.
  1714	\`\`\`
  1715	
  1716	**Detailed Example 23: First Law Application**
  1717	*A gas is heated with 1,000 J of heat and does 400 J of work. What is the change in internal energy?*
  1718	
  1719	\`\`\`
  1720	Step 1: Identify known values
  1721	Q = +1,000 J (heat added)
  1722	W = +400 J (work done by system)
  1723	
  1724	Step 2: Apply first law
  1725	ΔU = Q - W
  1726	ΔU = 1,000 - 400 = 600 J
  1727	
  1728	Answer: 600 J increase in internal energy
  1729	\`\`\`
  1730	
  1731	**Detailed Example 24: Carnot Efficiency**
  1732	*Calculate the maximum efficiency of a heat engine operating between 500°C and 100°C.*
  1733	
  1734	\`\`\`
  1735	Step 1: Convert to Kelvin
  1736	T_hot = 500 + 273 = 773K
  1737	T_cold = 100 + 273 = 373K
  1738	
  1739	Step 2: Calculate efficiency
  1740	η = 1 - T_cold/T_hot
  1741	η = 1 - 373/773
  1742	η = 1 - 0.483
  1743	η = 0.517 = 51.7%
  1744	
  1745	Answer: 51.7%
  1746	\`\`\`
  1747	
  1748	---
  1749	
  1750	### Engineering Units and Conversions
  1751	
  1752	#### SI Base Units
  1753	
  1754	\`\`\`
  1755	Length: meter (m)
  1756	Mass: kilogram (kg)
  1757	Time: second (s)
  1758	Current: ampere (A)
  1759	Temperature: kelvin (K)
  1760	Amount: mole (mol)
  1761	Luminous Intensity: candela (cd)
  1762	\`\`\`
  1763	
  1764	#### Common Engineering Conversions
  1765	
  1766	\`\`\`
  1767	Length:
  1768	1 m = 3.281 ft
  1769	1 km = 0.621 miles
  1770	1 inch = 25.4 mm
  1771	1 mile = 1.609 km
  1772	
  1773	Area:
  1774	1 m² = 10.764 ft²
  1775	1 ft² = 0.093 m²
  1776	
  1777	Volume:
  1778	1 m³ = 1,000 liters
  1779	1 gallon (US) = 3.785 liters
  1780	1 gallon (UK) = 4.546 liters
  1781	1 m³ = 219.97 gallons (UK)
  1782	
  1783	Mass:
  1784	1 kg = 2.205 lb
  1785	1 ton (metric) = 1,000 kg
  1786	1 ton (US) = 907.185 kg
  1787	1 lb = 0.454 kg
  1788	
  1789	Force:
  1790	1 N = 0.225 lbf
  1791	1 kgf = 9.81 N
  1792	1 lbf = 4.448 N
  1793	
  1794	Pressure:
  1795	1 Pa = 1 N/m²
  1796	1 bar = 100 kPa
  1797	1 atm = 101.325 kPa
  1798	1 psi = 6.895 kPa
  1799	1 MPa = 10 bar
  1800	
  1801	Energy:
  1802	1 J = 1 N·m
  1803	1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J
  1804	1 cal = 4.184 J
  1805	1 BTU = 1,055.06 J
  1806	
  1807	Power:
  1808	1 W = 1 J/s
  1809	1 hp (metric) = 735.5 W
  1810	1 hp (mechanical) = 745.7 W
  1811	1 kW = 1.341 hp
  1812	
  1813	Temperature:
  1814	°F = °C × 1.8 + 32
  1815	°C = (°F - 32) / 1.8
  1816	K = °C + 273.15
  1817	°R = °F + 459.67
  1818	\`\`\`
  1819	
  1820	**Detailed Example 25: Unit Conversion Practice**
  1821	*Convert 100 km/h to m/s.*
  1822	
  1823	\`\`\`
  1824	100 km/h = 100,000 m/h
  1825	= 100,000 / 3600 m/s
  1826	= 27.78 m/s
  1827	
  1828	Or use conversion: 1 km/h = 0.2778 m/s
  1829	100 × 0.2778 = 27.78 m/s
  1830	
  1831	Answer: 27.78 m/s
  1832	\`\`\`
  1833	
  1834	**Detailed Example 26: Energy Conversion**
  1835	*Convert 5 kWh to Joules.*
  1836	
  1837	\`\`\`
  1838	5 kWh = 5 × 1,000 × 3,600 = 18,000,000 J = 18 MJ
  1839	Answer: 18 MJ
  1840	\`\`\`
  1841	
  1842	---
  1843	
  1844	### Practice Questions with Solutions
  1845	
  1846	**Fluid Mechanics:**
  1847	1. Calculate pressure at 15m depth in water (ρ=1000 kg/m³).
  1848	   *Solution: P = 1000 × 9.81 × 15 = 147,150 Pa = 147.15 kPa*
  1849	
  1850	2. Convert 3.5 bar to kPa.
  1851	   *Solution: 3.5 × 100 = 350 kPa*
  1852	
  1853	3. A fluid has density 850 kg/m³. What is its specific gravity?
  1854	   *Solution: SG = 850/1000 = 0.85*
  1855	
  1856	4. What is viscosity and why is it important?
  1857	   *Solution: Viscosity is resistance to flow. Important for pump selection, pipeline design, lubrication.*
  1858	
  1859	5. What is the difference between density and specific gravity?
  1860	   *Solution: Density is absolute value (kg/m³). Specific gravity is relative to water (dimensionless).*
  1861	
  1862	**Pumps:**
  1863	6. What is the main difference between centrifugal and positive displacement pumps?
  1864	   *Solution: Centrifugal uses impeller for continuous flow; positive displacement moves fixed volume per cycle.*
  1865	
  1866	7. For what applications would you use a positive displacement pump?
  1867	   *Solution: High viscosity fluids, high pressure, precise flow control, metering.*
  1868	
  1869	8. When is a centrifugal pump most suitable?
  1870	   *Solution: Low viscosity fluids, high flow rates, clean fluids.*
  1871	
  1872	9. Calculate pump power: Q=0.05 m³/s, H=30m, ρ=1000 kg/m³, η=75%.
  1873	   *Solution: P = (0.05 × 30 × 1000 × 9.81)/(0.75 × 1000) = 14,715/750 = 19.62 kW*
  1874	
  1875	**Compressors:**
  1876	10. What is the purpose of an intercooler?
  1877	    *Solution: To cool air between compressor stages, reducing compression work and preventing overheating.*
  1878	
  1879	11. What is the purpose of an aftercooler?
  1880	    *Solution: To cool air after final compression to remove moisture for distribution.*
  1881	
  1882	12. What is the difference between an intercooler and aftercooler?
  1883	    *Solution: Intercooler is between stages; aftercooler is after the final stage.*
  1884	
  1885	**Heat Transfer:**
  1886	13. What are the three modes of heat transfer?
  1887	    *Solution: Conduction, convection, radiation.*
  1888	
  1889	14. Give an example of each mode of heat transfer.
  1890	    *Solution: Conduction - hot pan handle; Convection - boiling water; Radiation - sun warming earth.*
  1891	
  1892	15. Why is air a poor conductor of heat?
  1893	    *Solution: Low thermal conductivity (0.026 W/m·K) because molecules are far apart.*
  1894	
  1895	16. How does convection differ from conduction?
  1896	    *Solution: Conduction is through direct contact; convection involves fluid movement.*
  1897	
  1898	**Efficiency:**
  1899	17. Calculate efficiency if input is 2kW and output is 1.6kW.
  1900	    *Solution: η = 1.6/2 × 100 = 80%*
  1901	
  1902	18. If a machine is 75% efficient and produces 30W of output, what is input power?
  1903	    *Solution: Input = 30/0.75 = 40W*
  1904	
  1905	19. What are the common causes of inefficiency in machines?
  1906	    *Solution: Friction, heat loss, sound, vibration, wear.*
  1907	
  1908	**Mechanical Advantage:**
  1909	20. A lever with effort arm 3m and load arm 1m. What is MA?
  1910	    *Solution: MA = 3/1 = 3*
  1911	
  1912	21. What does a mechanical advantage of 5 mean?
  1913	    *Solution: You can lift 5 times the force you apply (5N effort lifts 25N load).*
  1914	
  1915	**Materials:**
  1916	22. What is the difference between ductility and toughness?
  1917	    *Solution: Ductility is ability to deform without fracture; toughness is ability to absorb energy before fracture.*
  1918	
  1919	23. Name two methods to prevent corrosion.
  1920	    *Solution: Coating (paint, plating), cathodic protection.*
  1921	
  1922	24. What type of material is carbon fiber?
  1923	    *Solution: Composite material.*
  1924	
  1925	**Thermodynamics:**
  1926	25. What is the first law of thermodynamics?
  1927	    *Solution: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted from one form to another.*
  1928	
  1929	26. What is entropy?
  1930	    *Solution: Measure of disorder in a system. Always increases in isolated systems.*
  1931	
  1932	27. Why can't heat spontaneously flow from cold to hot?
  1933	    *Solution: Second law of thermodynamics - entropy would decrease, which is impossible.*
  1934	
  1935	**Conversions:**
  1936	28. Convert 2.5 kW to hp.
  1937	    *Solution: 2.5 × 1.341 = 3.35 hp*
  1938	
  1939	29. Convert 100°C to °F.
  1940	    *Solution: 100 × 1.8 + 32 = 212°F*
  1941	
  1942	30. Convert 150 psi to kPa.
  1943	    *Solution: 150 × 6.895 = 1,034.25 kPa*
  1944	
  1945	31. Convert 50 m³/h to L/s.
  1946	    *Solution: 50 × 1000 / 3600 = 13.89 L/s*
  1947	
  1948	---
  1949	
  1950	### Quick Reference: General Engineering Formulas
  1951	
  1952	| Quantity | Formula | Units |
  1953	|----------|---------|-------|
  1954	| Pressure (static) | P = ρgh | Pa |
  1955	| Pressure | P = F/A | Pa |
  1956	| Force | F = P × A | N |
  1957	| Density | ρ = m/V | kg/m³ |
  1958	| Specific Gravity | SG = ρ/ρ_water | - |
  1959	| Power (pump) | P = QHρg/η | W |
  1960	| Efficiency | η = Output/Input | % |
  1961	| Mechanical Advantage (lever) | MA = d_effort/d_load | - |
  1962	| Mechanical Advantage (pulley) | MA = Number of rope segments | - |
  1963	| Heat Conduction | Q = -kA(ΔT/L) | W |
  1964	| Heat Convection | Q = hA(T_s - T_f) | W |
  1965	| Heat Radiation | Q = εσAT⁴ | W |
  1966	| Carnot Efficiency | η = 1 - T_c/T_h | % |
  1967	| First Law | ΔU = Q - W | J |
  1968	
  1969	---
  1970	
  1971	### Common Engineering Mistakes
  1972	
  1973	 **Wrong**: Confusing pressure and force
  1974	 **Correct**: Pressure = Force/Area. Pressure is force per unit area.
  1975	
  1976	 **Wrong**: Using wrong units in calculations
  1977	 **Correct**: Always check and convert units. Use consistent unit systems.
  1978	
  1979	 **Wrong**: Assuming all pumps work for all fluids
  1980	 **Correct**: Consider fluid viscosity, corrosiveness, and temperature when selecting pumps.
  1981	
  1982	 **Wrong**: Thinking heat and temperature are the same
  1983	 **Correct**: Heat is energy transfer; temperature is a measure of average kinetic energy.
  1984	
  1985	 **Wrong**: Confusing work and power
  1986	 **Correct**: Work = Force × Distance; Power = Work/Time.
  1987	
  1988	 **Wrong**: Ignoring friction losses
  1989	 **Correct**: Always account for friction in real systems; theoretical calculations are ideal.
  1990	
  1991	 **Wrong**: Confusing mechanical advantage and efficiency
  1992	 **Correct**: MA is force amplification; efficiency is input-output ratio.
  1993	
  1994	 **Wrong**: Forgetting to convert Celsius to Kelvin in thermodynamics
  1995	 **Correct**: Always use Kelvin for thermodynamic calculations (K = °C + 273.15).
  1996	`
  1997	    },
  1998	    {
  1999	      id: 'oil-gas',
  2000	      title: 'Oil & Gas Engineering',
  2001	      content: `
  2002	### Introduction to Oil & Gas
  2003	
  2004	The oil and gas industry is divided into three main sectors:
  2005	1. **Upstream**: Exploration and Production (E&P) - Finding and extracting
  2006	2. **Midstream**: Transportation and Storage - Moving and storing
  2007	3. **Downstream**: Refining and Marketing - Processing and selling
  2008	
  2009	---
  2010	
  2011	### Upstream Operations
  2012	
  2013	#### Exploration
  2014	
  2015	The process of finding oil and gas reserves.
  2016	
  2017	**Methods:**
  2018	\`\`\`
  2019	1. Geological Studies:
  2020	   - Surface mapping (rock formations)
  2021	   - Core sampling (take rock samples)
  2022	   - Fossil analysis (indicates past environments)
  2023	   - Structural geology (trap identification)
  2024	
  2025	2. Geophysical Methods:
  2026	   - Seismic surveys (2D, 3D, 4D) - Most common
  2027	   - Gravity surveys (density differences)
  2028	   - Magnetic surveys (rock magnetism)
  2029	   - Electromagnetic surveys (conductivity)
  2030	
  2031	3. Exploration Drilling:
  2032	   - Wildcat wells (first well in new area)
  2033	   - Appraisal wells (confirm and delineate discovery)
  2034	   - Delineation wells (define reservoir extent)
  2035	\`\`\`
  2036	
  2037	**Seismic Survey Principles:**
  2038	\`\`\`
  2039	- Sound waves are sent into the earth (using vibrators or explosives)
  2040	- Waves reflect back from rock layers (at interfaces)
  2041	- Recorded by geophones or hydrophones
  2042	- Processed and interpreted to create subsurface images
  2043	- Different rock types reflect differently (velocity and density contrast)
  2044	
  2045	4D Seismic:
  2046	- Repeated 3D surveys over time
  2047	- Monitors reservoir changes
  2048	- Tracks fluid movement
  2049	- Identifies bypassed oil
  2050	\`\`\`
  2051	
  2052	#### Drilling
  2053	
  2054	**Drilling Rig Components:**
  2055	\`\`\`
  2056	1. Derrick/Mast: Vertical support structure for drill string
  2057	2. Draw works: Hoisting mechanism with drum and cable
  2058	3. Drill string: Drill pipe + bottom hole assembly + drill bit
  2059	4. Mud system: Circulates drilling fluid (mud pumps, tanks, shakers)
  2060	5. BOP (Blowout Preventer): Multiple rams to seal well in emergency
  2061	6. Kelly/Swivel: Top drive or rotary table rotates drill string
  2062	
  2063	Other Components:
  2064	- Crown block and traveling block (pulley system)
  2065	- Hook (connects traveling block to swivel)
  2066	- Swivel (allows rotation while pumping mud)
  2067	- Mud motor (downhole motor for directional drilling)
  2068	\`\`\`
  2069	
  2070	**Drilling Fluid (Mud) Functions:**
  2071	\`\`\`
  2072	1. Remove cuttings from wellbore (carry to surface)
  2073	2. Cool and lubricate drill bit (prevents overheating)
  2074	3. Control formation pressure (prevent blowouts)
  2075	4. Stabilize wellbore (prevent collapse)
  2076	5. Transmit hydraulic energy to bit (power)
  2077	6. Provide information about formation (logging while drilling)
  2078	7. Prevent corrosion of drill string
  2079	8. Suspend cuttings when circulation stops
  2080	\`\`\`
  2081	
  2082	**Types of Drilling Rigs:**
  2083	\`\`\`
  2084	Onshore:
  2085	- Conventional land rigs (for accessible areas)
  2086	- Mobile land rigs (truck or trailer mounted)
  2087	
  2088	Offshore:
  2089	- Jack-up rigs (water depth up to 150m)
  2090	- Semi-submersible (deep water, 150-3,000m)
  2091	- Drillship (ultra-deep water, up to 3,600m+)
  2092	- Fixed platform (shallow water, up to 300m)
  2093	- Tension leg platform (TLP) (deep water)
  2094	- SPAR platform (very deep water)
  2095	\`\`\`
  2096	
  2097	---
  2098	
  2099	### Production
  2100	
  2101	#### Primary Recovery
  2102	
  2103	\`\`\`
  2104	Uses natural reservoir energy:
  2105	- Natural pressure (gas cap drive)
  2106	- Water influx (water drive)
  2107	- Solution gas drive (gas comes out of solution)
  2108	- Gravity drainage (oil flows downhill)
  2109	
  2110	Recovery Factor: 5-15% of OOIP (Original Oil In Place)
  2111	
  2112	Drive Mechanisms:
  2113	- Solution Gas Drive: Gas expands as pressure drops
  2114	- Gas Cap Drive: Expanding gas cap pushes oil
  2115	- Water Drive: Water influx from aquifer
  2116	- Gravity Drainage: Oil flows downward to wells
  2117	\`\`\`
  2118	
  2119	**Detailed Example 1: Primary Recovery**
  2120	*An oil reservoir has 100 million barrels of oil initially in place (OOIP). Primary recovery is expected to recover 10%. How many barrels will be recovered?*
  2121	
  2122	\`\`\`
  2123	Recovery = 100,000,000 × 0.10 = 10,000,000 barrels
  2124	Answer: 10 million barrels
  2125	\`\`\`
  2126	
  2127	#### Secondary Recovery
  2128	
  2129	\`\`\`
  2130	Introduced when natural pressure declines:
  2131	- Water injection (water flooding) - Most common
  2132	- Gas injection (gas flooding)
  2133	
  2134	Recovery Factor: Up to 40% (additional 15-25%)
  2135	
  2136	Water Injection Process:
  2137	1. Water is treated (filtered, oxygen removed)
  2138	2. Injected into reservoir via injection wells
  2139	3. Sweeps oil towards production wells
  2140	4. Maintains reservoir pressure
  2141	5. Improves displacement efficiency
  2142	
  2143	Patterns:
  2144	- Five-spot (one injector, four producers)
  2145	- Seven-spot (one injector, six producers)
  2146	- Nine-spot (one injector, eight producers)
  2147	\`\`\`
  2148	
  2149	**Detailed Example 2: Secondary Recovery**
  2150	*If primary recovery recovered 10 million barrels from a 100 million barrel reservoir, and secondary recovery recovers an additional 20%, what is total recovered?*
  2151	
  2152	\`\`\`
  2153	Secondary recovery = 100 × 0.20 = 20 million barrels
  2154	Total = 10 + 20 = 30 million barrels
  2155	Answer: 30 million barrels (30% recovery)
  2156	\`\`\`
  2157	
  2158	#### Enhanced Oil Recovery (EOR)
  2159	
  2160	\`\`\`
  2161	Used after primary and secondary recovery:
  2162	
  2163	1. Thermal EOR:
  2164	   - Steam injection (most common)
  2165	   - In-situ combustion (fire flooding)
  2166	   - Cyclic steam stimulation (huff and puff)
  2167	
  2168	2. Gas EOR:
  2169	   - CO₂ injection (miscible)
  2170	   - Nitrogen injection (immiscible)
  2171	   - Hydrocarbon gas injection
  2172	
  2173	3. Chemical EOR:
  2174	   - Polymer flooding (increases viscosity of water)
  2175	   - Surfactant flooding (reduces interfacial tension)
  2176	   - Alkaline flooding (reacts with acids in oil)
  2177	
  2178	Recovery Factor: Additional 10-20% (Total up to 60%)
  2179	
  2180	Selection Criteria:
  2181	- Reservoir temperature and pressure
  2182	- Oil viscosity
  2183	- Permeability
  2184	- Oil composition
  2185	- Economics (CO₂ injection can be expensive)
  2186	\`\`\`
  2187	
  2188	**Detailed Example 3: Total Recovery Factor**
  2189	*If primary recovery is 10%, secondary adds 15%, and EOR adds 10%, what is the total recovery factor?*
  2190	
  2191	\`\`\`
  2192	Total = 10% + 15% + 10% = 35%
  2193	Answer: 35% of OOIP
  2194	\`\`\`
  2195	
  2196	**Detailed Example 4: Oil Recovery Calculation**
  2197	*A 500 million barrel reservoir has: primary recovery 12%, secondary recovery 18%, EOR recovery 10%. How many barrels are recoverable total?*
  2198	
  2199	\`\`\`
  2200	Total RF = 12 + 18 + 10 = 40%
  2201	Recoverable = 500 × 0.40 = 200 million barrels
  2202	Answer: 200 million barrels
  2203	\`\`\`
  2204	
  2205	---
  2206	
  2207	### Wellhead and Christmas Tree
  2208	
  2209	#### Wellhead Components
  2210	
  2211	\`\`\`
  2212	Functions:
  2213	- Support the wellbore (casing weight)
  2214	- Provide pressure control (seal against formation)
  2215	- Permit access to the well (workover operations)
  2216	- Enable installation of equipment (Christmas tree)
  2217	- Provide connections for surface facilities
  2218	
  2219	Components:
  2220	- Casing head: Supports casing string
  2221	- Tubing head: Supports tubing string
  2222	- Spool: Provides space for valves
  2223	- Bowl: Holds casing hanger
  2224	- Connectors: Join different components
  2225	\`\`\`
  2226	
  2227	#### Christmas Tree
  2228	
  2229	\`\`\`
  2230	The assembly of valves on the wellhead for production control.
  2231	
  2232	Components:
  2233	1. Master valve (main shutoff)
  2234	2. Wing valves (flow control)
  2235	3. Swab valve (access for operations)
  2236	4. Choke (flow restriction)
  2237	5. Pressure gauges (monitoring)
  2238	6. Injection ports (for chemical injection)
  2239	
  2240	Functions:
  2241	- Control flow from well
  2242	- Provide access for maintenance
  2243	- Allow injection of fluids
  2244	- Monitor well conditions
  2245	- Emergency shutdown capability
  2246	
  2247	Tree Types:
  2248	- Conventional tree (vertical)
  2249	- Horizontal tree (spool tree)
  2250	- Subsea tree (underwater)
  2251	\`\`\`
  2252	
  2253	**Detailed Example 5: Christmas Tree Valve Functions**
  2254	*What is the purpose of the choke on a Christmas tree?*
  2255	
  2256	\`\`\`
  2257	Answer: To regulate and restrict the flow of produced fluids from the well, controlling flow rate and maintaining back pressure.
  2258	\`\`\`
  2259	
  2260	**Detailed Example 6: Emergency Shutdown**
  2261	*What valve would you close first in an emergency?*
  2262	
  2263	\`\`\`
  2264	Answer: The master valve (main shutoff) to isolate the well
  2265	\`\`\`
  2266	
  2267	---
  2268	
  2269	### Separators
  2270	
  2271	Separators are vessels that separate produced fluids into their constituent phases.
  2272	
  2273	#### Two-Phase Separators
  2274	
  2275	\`\`\`
  2276	Separates: Gas and Liquids (one liquid phase)
  2277	
  2278	Stages:
  2279	1. Inlet diverter (initial bulk separation)
  2280	2. Gravity separation section (liquid drops out)
  2281	3. Mist extractor (removes liquid droplets from gas)
  2282	4. Liquid collection section (accumulates liquid)
  2283	5. Gas outlet (top of vessel)
  2284	6. Liquid outlet (bottom of vessel)
  2285	
  2286	Applications:
  2287	- Well testing
  2288	- Small production systems
  2289	- When water content is minimal
  2290	- Gas wells (with some liquid)
  2291	\`\`\`
  2292	
  2293	#### Three-Phase Separators
  2294	
  2295	\`\`\`
  2296	Separates: Oil, Gas, and Water
  2297	
  2298	Stages:
  2299	1. Inlet diverter (initial separation)
  2300	2. Gas/liquid separation (bulk)
  2301	3. Oil/water separation (gravity settling)
  2302	4. Oil weir (controls oil level)
  2303	5. Water outlet (bottom of vessel)
  2304	6. Oil outlet (middle of vessel)
  2305	7. Gas outlet (top of vessel)
  2306	
  2307	Applications:
  2308	- Production facilities
  2309	- Where water is present in production
  2310	- Most production systems
  2311	\`\`\`
  2312	
  2313	**Detailed Example 7: Separator Selection**
  2314	*What type of separator would be used if the produced fluids contain oil, gas, and water?*
  2315	
  2316	\`\`\`
  2317	Answer: Three-phase separator
  2318	\`\`\`
  2319	
  2320	**Separation Principles:**
  2321	\`\`\`
  2322	1. Gravity Separation:
  2323	   - Different densities cause phases to separate
  2324	   - Heavier phases settle to the bottom
  2325	   - Lighter phases rise to the top
  2326	   - Stokes' Law governs settling velocity
  2327	
  2328	2. Centrifugal Separation:
  2329	   - Uses rotational force (cyclone effect)
  2330	   - More efficient than gravity
  2331	   - Used in compact systems
  2332	   - For gas/liquid separation
  2333	
  2334	3. Coalescence:
  2335	   - Small droplets combine to form larger ones
  2336	   - Speeds up separation
  2337	   - Used in mist extractors
  2338	   - Plate packs increase coalescence
  2339	
  2340	4. Flotation:
  2341	   - Bubbles attach to oil droplets
  2342	   - Raises them to surface
  2343	   - Used in produced water treatment
  2344	\`\`\`
  2345	
  2346	---
  2347	
  2348	### Pipelines
  2349	
  2350	Pipelines transport oil, gas, and other products over long distances.
  2351	
  2352	#### Pipeline Types
  2353	
  2354	\`\`\`
  2355	1. Flowlines: Wellhead to processing facility (short, small diameter)
  2356	2. Gathering Lines: Multiple wells to central facility (medium)
  2357	3. Trunk Lines: Major transmission lines (long distance, large diameter)
  2358	4. Distribution Lines: Final delivery to consumers (small diameter)
  2359	
  2360	Pipeline Categories:
  2361	- Onshore pipelines (above ground or buried)
  2362	- Offshore pipelines (subsea)
  2363	- Product pipelines (refined products)
  2364	- Transmission pipelines (crude oil, natural gas)
  2365	\`\`\`
  2366	
  2367	#### Pipeline Components
  2368	
  2369	\`\`\`
  2370	1. Pipe (steel, HDPE, fiberglass)
  2371	2. Compressors/Pumps (maintain pressure)
  2372	3. Valves (block, control, safety, check)
  2373	4. Scraper traps (for PIG launching/receiving)
  2374	5. Corrosion protection (coatings, cathodic protection)
  2375	6. SCADA systems (monitoring and control)
  2376	7. Cathodic protection (anodes, rectifiers)
  2377	8. Pig launcher/receiver (for PIG operations)
  2378	9. Metering stations (measure flow)
  2379	10. Heating systems (for waxy crude)
  2380	\`\`\`
  2381	
  2382	#### PIG (Pipeline Inspection Gauge)
  2383	
  2384	\`\`\`
  2385	Types:
  2386	1. Cleaning PIGs:
  2387	   - Remove debris
  2388	   - Scrape deposits
  2389	   - Rust removal
  2390	   - Paraffin/wax removal
  2391	
  2392	2. Inspection PIGs (Intelligent PIGs):
  2393	   - Measure wall thickness (UT, magnetic flux)
  2394	   - Detect corrosion and pitting
  2395	   - Find cracks
  2396	   - GPS tracking
  2397	
  2398	3. Batching PIGs:
  2399	   - Separate different products
  2400	   - Minimize mixing
  2401	   - Reduce contamination
  2402	
  2403	Functions:
  2404	- Clean pipeline interior
  2405	- Inspect for defects
  2406	- Separate product batches
  2407	- Remove water/debris
  2408	- Run regularly for maintenance
  2409	\`\`\`
  2410	
  2411	**Detailed Example 8: PIG Operation**
  2412	*What is the purpose of running a PIG through a pipeline?*
  2413	
  2414	\`\`\`
  2415	Answer: To clean the pipeline, inspect for damage, or separate different products being transported
  2416	\`\`\`
  2417	
  2418	#### Cathodic Protection
  2419	
  2420	\`\`\`
  2421	Prevents corrosion of metal pipelines.
  2422	
  2423	Methods:
  2424	1. Sacrificial Anode (Galvanic):
  2425	   - More reactive metal connected to pipeline
  2426	   - Anode corrodes instead of pipe
  2427	   - Zinc, magnesium, aluminum anodes
  2428	   - No external power required
  2429	   - Simple and reliable
  2430	
  2431	2. Impressed Current:
  2432	   - External DC power supply (rectifier)
  2433	   - Forced protection
  2434	   - Used for large pipelines
  2435	   - Anodes are inert (graphite, platinum)
  2436	   - More expensive but longer protection
  2437	
  2438	Benefits:
  2439	- Extends pipeline life
  2440	- Prevents leaks
  2441	- Reduces maintenance
  2442	- Cost-effective
  2443	- Environmental protection
  2444	\`\`\`
  2445	
  2446	**Detailed Example 9: Cathodic Protection**
  2447	*What is the purpose of cathodic protection in pipelines?*
  2448	
  2449	\`\`\`
  2450	Answer: To prevent corrosion of the pipeline metal by making it the cathode in an electrochemical cell
  2451	\`\`\`
  2452	
  2453	---
  2454	
  2455	### Safety & HSSE
  2456	
  2457	HSSE = Health, Safety, Security, and Environment
  2458	
  2459	#### Permit to Work (PTW)
  2460	
  2461	\`\`\`
  2462	Purpose: 
  2463	- Authorize high-risk work
  2464	- Identify hazards
  2465	- Implement controls
  2466	- Ensure safety
  2467	- Coordinate multiple activities
  2468	
  2469	Types:
  2470	1. Routine Permits (minor, low-risk work)
  2471	2. Hot Work Permits (welding, grinding, cutting - ignition sources)
  2472	3. Cold Work Permits (non-hazardous, no ignition)
  2473	4. Confined Space Permits (vessels, tanks, pits)
  2474	5. Excavation Permits (digging)
  2475	6. Radiation Permits (NORM, industrial radiography)
  2476	
  2477	PTW Process:
  2478	1. Request permit (identify work scope)
  2479	2. Identify hazards (risk assessment)
  2480	3. Implement controls (mitigation measures)
  2481	4. Authorize work (supervisor approval)
  2482	5. Perform work (follow procedures)
  2483	6. Close out permit (confirm completion)
  2484	\`\`\`
  2485	
  2486	**Detailed Example 10: Permit to Work**
  2487	*When is a hot work permit required?*
  2488	
  2489	\`\`\`
  2490	Answer: Any work that could create a source of ignition (welding, grinding, flame cutting, spark-producing tools)
  2491	\`\`\`
  2492	
  2493	#### Safety Equipment
  2494	
  2495	\`\`\`
  2496	1. Personal Protective Equipment (PPE):
  2497	   - Hard hat (protects from falling objects)
  2498	   - Safety glasses (eye protection)
  2499	   - Coveralls (full body protection)
  2500	   - Safety boots (steel toe, slip resistant)
  2501	   - Gloves (cut resistant, chemical resistant)
  2502	   - Ear protection (hearing protection)
  2503	   - Fall protection (harness, lanyard)
  2504	
  2505	2. Safety Systems:
  2506	   - Fire and gas detection
  2507	   - Emergency shutdown (ESD) system
  2508	   - Blowout preventer (BOP)
  2509	   - Relief valves
  2510	   - Fire suppression systems
  2511	   - Emergency response equipment
  2512	\`\`\`
  2513	
  2514	#### Environmental Protection
  2515	
  2516	\`\`\`
  2517	Main Environmental Risks:
  2518	- Oil spills (marine and terrestrial)
  2519	- Gas flaring (air pollution)
  2520	- Produced water (water contamination)
  2521	- Drilling waste (mud and cuttings)
  2522	- Air emissions (NOx, SOx, CO₂)
  2523	
  2524	Protection Measures:
  2525	- Spill response plans (oil spill contingency)
  2526	- Waste management (proper disposal)
  2527	- Emission controls (flare reduction)
  2528	- Environmental monitoring (regular testing)
  2529	- Produced water treatment (clean before discharge)
  2530	- Gas reinjection (reduce flaring)
  2531	- Zero discharge policies
  2532	\`\`\`
  2533	
  2534	**Detailed Example 11: Environmental Protection**
  2535	*What are the main environmental risks in oil and gas operations?*
  2536	
  2537	\`\`\`
  2538	Answer: Oil spills, gas flaring, produced water discharge, drilling waste, and air emissions
  2539	\`\`\`
  2540	
  2541	---
  2542	
  2543	### Basic Instrumentation
  2544	
  2545	#### Types of Instruments
  2546	
  2547	**Pressure Measurement:**
  2548	\`\`\`
  2549	1. Pressure Transmitter (Smart/Electronic):
  2550	   - 4-20mA or 0-10V output
  2551	   - Converts pressure to electrical signal
  2552	   - HART or FOUNDATION Fieldbus communication
  2553	   - Used in control systems (DCS/PLC)
  2554	
  2555	2. Pressure Gauge:
  2556	   - Local reading only
  2557	   - Bourdon tube, diaphragm, capsule types
  2558	   - No electrical output
  2559	   - Accuracy: 0.5-2% full scale
  2560	
  2561	3. Pressure Switch:
  2562	   - Opens/closes contacts at set pressure
  2563	   - Used for alarms and interlocks
  2564	   - Mechanical or electronic
  2565	\`\`\`
  2566	
  2567	**Detailed Example 12: Pressure Transmitter Output**
  2568	*A pressure transmitter has a range of 0-100 bar and output of 4-20mA. What is the output at 50 bar?*
  2569	
  2570	\`\`\`
  2571	Step 1: Calculate percentage of range
  2572	50 bar / 100 bar = 50% = 0.5
  2573	
  2574	Step 2: Calculate output signal
  2575	Output = 4mA + (0.5 × 16mA)
  2576	Output = 4mA + 8mA = 12mA
  2577	
  2578	Answer: 12 mA
  2579	\`\`\`
  2580	
  2581	**Temperature Measurement:**
  2582	\`\`\`
  2583	1. Thermocouple:
  2584	   - Two dissimilar metals (junction)
  2585	   - Generates voltage based on temperature difference
  2586	   - Types: J (iron-constantan), K (chromel-alumel), T (copper-constantan), E, R, S
  2587	   - Range: -200°C to 1,800°C (depends on type)
  2588	   - Accuracy: ±0.5-1.5°C
  2589	
  2590	2. RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector):
  2591	   - Platinum element (Pt100)
  2592	   - Resistance changes with temperature (0.385Ω/°C typical)
  2593	   - Accurate and stable
  2594	   - Range: -200°C to 850°C
  2595	   - Accuracy: ±0.1-0.5°C
  2596	
  2597	3. Thermowell:
  2598	   - Protects temperature sensor
  2599	   - Allows removal while under pressure
  2600	   - Made of corrosion-resistant material
  2601	   - Provides thermal contact
  2602	\`\`\`
  2603	
  2604	**Detailed Example 13: RTD Resistance**
  2605	*What is the resistance of a Pt100 RTD at 100°C?*
  2606	
  2607	\`\`\`
  2608	At 0°C, R = 100Ω
  2609	At 100°C, R = 100 × (1 + 0.00385 × 100) = 100 × 1.385 = 138.5Ω
  2610	Answer: 138.5Ω (approximately)
  2611	\`\`\`
  2612	
  2613	**Level Measurement:**
  2614	\`\`\`
  2615	1. Ultrasonic Level:
  2616	   - Sound waves measure distance
  2617	   - Non-contact
  2618	   - For liquids and solids
  2619	   - Range: 0.5-20m
  2620	
  2621	2. Radar Level:
  2622	   - Electromagnetic waves
  2623	   - Accurate in harsh conditions
  2624	   - Non-contact
  2625	   - Range: 0.5-30m
  2626	
  2627	3. DP Level (Differential Pressure):
  2628	   - Measures pressure difference
  2629	   - Direct and indirect methods
  2630	   - Simple and reliable
  2631	   - Range: 0-100m
  2632	
  2633	4. Float Switches:
  2634	   - Simple on/off control
  2635	   - Uses buoyancy
  2636	   - Low cost
  2637	   - Reliable
  2638	\`\`\`
  2639	
  2640	**Flow Measurement:**
  2641	\`\`\`
  2642	1. Flow Transmitter:
  2643	   - 4-20mA output
  2644	   - Coriolis, vortex, magnetic, ultrasonic
  2645	   - Varies with flow rate
  2646	
  2647	2. Flow Meter Types:
  2648	   - Orifice plate: Pressure drop method (DP)
  2649	   - Magnetic: Conductive fluids only
  2650	   - Coriolis: Mass flow (most accurate)
  2651	   - Ultrasonic: Non-invasive
  2652	   - Turbine: Mechanical measurement
  2653	   - Vortex: Vortex shedding frequency
  2654	
  2655	3. Flow Switch:
  2656	   - On/off output
  2657	   - Set point for flow
  2658	   - Protection applications (no-flow detection)
  2659	\`\`\`
  2660	
  2661	**Detailed Example 14: Flow Transmitter Output**
  2662	*A flow transmitter has range 0-100 m³/h and output 4-20mA. What is flow at 16mA?*
  2663	
  2664	\`\`\`
  2665	Step 1: Calculate percentage
  2666	(16mA - 4mA) / 16mA = 12/16 = 0.75 = 75%
  2667	
  2668	Step 2: Calculate flow
  2669	Flow = 0.75 × 100 = 75 m³/h
  2670	
  2671	Answer: 75 m³/h
  2672	\`\`\`
  2673	
  2674	---
  2675	
  2676	### Practice Questions with Solutions
  2677	
  2678	**Exploration and Production:**
  2679	1. What is the main difference between exploration and production?
  2680	   *Solution: Exploration finds oil/gas; production extracts it from the reservoir.*
  2681
